title: feat(domain/settings): cascade resolver for tasks + evidence + inventory
description:
## Problem / goal
Spec §06 "Evidence policy inheritance", §"State machine" ("State transitions"), §"Skipping and cancellation", and §08 "Consumption on task completion" all reference a settings cascade with the canonical keys:

- `evidence.policy` (W/P/U/E/T scopes — `forbid` absolute, otherwise most-specific wins)
- `tasks.checklist_required` (W/E/T)
- `tasks.allow_skip_with_reason` (W/E)
- `inventory.apply_on_task` (W/T)

The cd-7am7 completion service exposes injectable resolvers (`EvidencePolicyResolver`, `ChecklistRequiredResolver`, `SkipAllowedResolver`, `InventoryApplyHook`) with narrow defaults (read `Occurrence.photo_evidence` directly, return True/permissive). The full cascade (workspace -> property -> unit -> work-engagement -> task) is not yet implemented.

## Scope
- Build the §02 "Settings cascade" generic resolver (key, scope chain, value).
- Persist concrete settings rows on the matching scope tables.
- Implement `evidence.policy` with the §06 "forbid first, otherwise most specific wins" rule.
- Implement `tasks.checklist_required`, `tasks.allow_skip_with_reason`, `inventory.apply_on_task` as straight most-specific-wins resolvers.
- Wire the four resolvers into the completion service's defaults so the narrow today-defaults disappear.
- Update tests to cover the cascade behaviour (especially the `forbid`-absolute branch for evidence).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Generic settings cascade resolver exists
- [ ] Four canonical keys above are read through it
- [ ] `evidence.policy = forbid` at any layer wins absolutely
- [ ] Completion service no longer has narrow per-key defaults — they read the cascade
- [ ] Unit + integration tests cover both the happy path and forbid-absolute

## Why file this now
Filed alongside cd-7am7 — the narrow defaults are a deliberate bridge; this is the task that retires them. Multiple downstream services (turnover assignment, NL intake, agent runtime) will share the same resolver.

title: feat(db/assets): asset + asset_action v1 slice
description:
## Problem / goal
Spec §06 "Completing a task" #4 says: "If `asset_action_id` is set, update `asset_action.last_performed_at` = completed_at and `asset_action.last_performed_task_id` = task.id (§21)."

The cd-7am7 completion service exposes an `AssetActionHook` injection seam with a no-op default because neither `asset` nor `asset_action` is in the schema yet, and `occurrence.asset_action_id` is also missing. Completions today silently skip the asset-action stamp.

## Scope
- New Alembic migration creating:
  - `asset` (id, workspace_id FK, name, kind, property_id, area_id, model_md, serial, purchased_at, deleted_at, …)
  - `asset_action` (id, asset_id FK, name, frequency, last_performed_at, last_performed_task_id soft-ref, …)
- Add `occurrence.asset_id` and `occurrence.asset_action_id` columns (soft FKs).
- New SQLAlchemy models.
- Wire the real `AssetActionHook` body to update `last_performed_*` when the task carries `asset_action_id`.
- Update `tests/unit/tasks/test_completion.py` with an assertion that the stamp lands.

This is a v1 slice — the full §21 surface (asset history graph, depreciation, transfer) is out of scope.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `asset` and `asset_action` tables exist with the v1 columns
- [ ] `occurrence.asset_action_id` column exists
- [ ] Completion stamps `last_performed_at` + `last_performed_task_id` when set
- [ ] Hook default points at the real body
- [ ] Tests cover the asset-action stamp

## Why file this now
Filed alongside cd-7am7 — the no-op hook is a known gap; this task closes it without rewriting completion.

title: feat(db/tasks): task_completion tombstone table
description:
## Problem / goal
Spec §06 "Completing a task" #5 mandates a `task_completion` tombstone row written on every completion: "Insert `task_completion` row (a tombstone used for history even if the task is later edited)."

The cd-7am7 completion service exposes a `TombstoneHook` injection seam with a no-op default because the `task_completion` table is not in the schema yet. Completions today land but no tombstone survives if the task is later edited.

## Scope
- New Alembic migration creating `task_completion`:
  - `id` (ULID PK)
  - `workspace_id` (FK)
  - `occurrence_id` (FK, ON DELETE SET NULL — history survives task delete)
  - `completed_at`, `completed_by_user_id`, `completion_note_md`
  - `evidence_blob_hashes` (JSON list of attached photos at completion time)
  - `checklist_snapshot_json` (frozen view of checked items)
  - `created_at`
- New SQLAlchemy model.
- Wire the real `TombstoneHook` body in `app/domain/tasks/completion.py` to insert one row per call.
- Update `tests/unit/tasks/test_completion.py` to assert on the row.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `task_completion` table exists
- [ ] One row written per `complete()` call (regular + race winner + race loser)
- [ ] Hook default in completion service points at the real body
- [ ] Tests cover the tombstone

## Why file this now
Filed alongside cd-7am7 — the no-op default is a deliberate gap, not a partial implementation; this task closes it.

title: feat(db/tasks): add missing §06 state-machine columns
description:
## Problem / goal
Spec §06 "Task row" lists several columns the v1 `occurrence` table doesn't yet carry:

- `completion_note_md` — free-text note attached at completion time
- `skipped_reason` — distinct from `cancellation_reason` (skipped vs cancelled are two different terminal states with two different reasons)
- `overdue_since` — timestamp the worker stamped `state='overdue'`
- `due_by_utc` — SLA deadline; today the spec says default = `scheduled_for + duration` but the column is missing

cd-7am7 (completion service) bridged the gaps:
- `completion_note_md` -> persisted as an `Evidence` row of `kind='note'` (working but not the spec's shape)
- `skipped_reason` -> reused `cancellation_reason` (the audit action `task.skip` distinguishes skip from cancel; the column name is misleading)
- `overdue_since` -> `revert_overdue` accepts an in-memory `overdue` source state but cannot persist it (CHECK rejects)
- `due_by_utc` -> derived at SLA-check time from `scheduled_for`

## Scope
- New Alembic migration adding the four columns to `occurrence`.
- Widen the `occurrence.state` CHECK to include `overdue` (or paired with cd-7014).
- Migrate completion-service writes to the new columns:
  - `complete()` writes `completion_note_md` directly instead of an Evidence row.
  - `skip()` writes `skipped_reason` instead of `cancellation_reason`.
  - `revert_overdue()` clears `overdue_since` on transition out of `overdue`.
- Update the spec-bridge tests in `tests/unit/tasks/test_completion.py` to assert on the new columns.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Four new columns exist on `occurrence` with the spec-defined types
- [ ] Completion service writes the new columns (no more Evidence-note bridge)
- [ ] Skip vs cancel use distinct columns
- [ ] `overdue` is a legal CHECK value
- [ ] Tests updated to match

## Why file this now
Filed alongside cd-7am7 — the completion service compromises on schema gaps it cannot fix in scope. This is the followup that closes them.

title: feat(db/tasks): rename state enum 'done' -> 'completed' to match spec
description:
## Problem / goal
The DB CHECK constraint on `occurrence.state` (defined in `app/adapters/db/tasks/models.py` `_OCCURRENCE_STATE_VALUES`) carries `done` as the terminal-success state, but spec §06 "State machine" and every audit / event / docs reference uses `completed` (e.g. event name is `task.completed`, action is `task.complete`).

cd-7am7 (completion service) shipped using `done` as the persisted value because the CHECK rejects `completed`. The mismatch is invisible at runtime today but trips up readers and will trip up any future analytic / NL-generated SQL that joins by the spec's vocabulary.

## Scope
- New Alembic migration that ALTERs the CHECK constraint to swap `done` -> `completed`.
- Data migration: UPDATE every row `SET state='completed' WHERE state='done'`.
- Update `_OCCURRENCE_STATE_VALUES` and any string literals in the codebase (rg for `'done'`).
- Update `app/domain/tasks/completion.py` so `TaskStateName`, `_ALLOWED_EDGES`, and the `task.state = 'done'` write all use `'completed'`.
- Update unit tests' bootstrap helpers and assertions.
- Cross-link cd-7am7 in the migration's docstring.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] DB CHECK constraint accepts `completed` and rejects `done`
- [ ] Backfill migration converts every existing row
- [ ] Domain code, unit tests, integration tests all use `completed`
- [ ] No string-literal `'done'` left referencing task state (`rg "'done'" app/ tests/`)

## Why file this now
Filed alongside cd-7am7 — the completion service uses the (legal-but-wrong) `done` value today; this task swaps to the spec word in one shot.

title: Resolve §03 'Tight initial caps' vs §11 'Cap' spec drift on the ledger seed number
description:
## Problem

Spec §03 'Tight initial caps' says a freshly-signed-up workspace runs at **10 % of the free-tier caps** until it reaches `verification_state='human_verified'`. Spec §11 'Cap' says the prod default for `workspace_budget.cap_usd_30d` is **5.00 USD** (500 cents) seeded in the same transaction as the workspace row, with no explicit statement about the tight-cap phase scaling that number.

Before cd-wpyt the implementation honoured §11's literal reading — the ledger row got the full 500-cent cap while `workspace.quota_json['llm_budget_cents_30d']` got the tight 50-cent value. That meant an abusive freshly-signed-up workspace could burn 10× the intended spend until a human lifted the verification gate.

## What cd-wpyt applied

The safer interpretation — the ledger `cap_cents` is scaled by the same `_TIGHT_CAP_FRACTION` (10 %) as `quota_json` via the new `app.domain.plans.tight_cap_cents()` helper. The two representations of the cap now always agree on the number the workspace runs at during the tight-cap phase.

See `app/auth/signup.py::provision_workspace_and_owner_seat` and `app/domain/plans.py::tight_cap_cents`.

## What still needs human judgement

1. Should §11 explicitly call out the tight-cap phase and reference `tight_cap_cents`? (Probably yes — §11 currently reads as if the ledger cap is static at 500 until an operator tweaks it.)
2. Should §03 explicitly call out that the LLM budget cap is one of the quotas scaled? (Probably yes — §03 currently says 'LLM budget cap is 10 % of the free-tier cap' which is unambiguous, but §11 is the spec that operators read for LLM policy and doesn't cross-reference §03.)
3. When the lift-on-human-verification code lands (today there's no path that flips `verification_state → 'human_verified'`, let alone one that rewrites the ledger cap), both the ledger `cap_cents` and `quota_json['llm_budget_cents_30d']` need to lift together. Pin the lift path through a single helper so they can't drift again.

## Acceptance criteria

- Both §03 and §11 carry a cross-reference to the other on the tight-cap topic so a reader of either spec sees the full picture.
- The 'lift on human_verified' flow (when it lands) routes through one function that bumps both the ledger row and the workspace quota_json entry together.
- A regression test asserts the ledger `cap_cents` == `workspace.quota_json['llm_budget_cents_30d']` on a fresh signup.

## Context

- `app/auth/signup.py:170-173` — `FALLBACK_CAP_CENTS` docstring on the tight-cap scaling.
- `app/domain/plans.py::tight_cap_cents` — single-source-of-truth for the scaling.
- cd-tubi (this ticket's parent) seeded the ledger row in the signup transaction.
- cd-wpyt (self-review) applied the safer scaling interpretation.

title: feat(api): /admin/usage endpoint surfaces cd-wjpl telemetry columns
description:
## Problem / goal
cd-wjpl landed six new telemetry columns on `llm_usage`
(`assignment_id`, `fallback_attempts`, `finish_reason`,
`actor_user_id`, `token_id`, `agent_label`) plus the composite
index `ix_llm_usage_workspace_actor_created` that backs 'filter by
delegating user'. Nothing consumes the columns yet — they're
written by the recorder but no UI or API surfaces them.

## Technical context
- Spec: `docs/specs/11-llm-and-agents.md` §'Cost tracking — extended'
  (expected /admin/usage surfaces: per-capability breakdown,
  per-rung observability, filter by delegating user).
- Spec: `docs/specs/11-llm-and-agents.md` §'Agent audit trail' (the
  agent-activity view filters `actor_kind = 'agent' OR agent_label
  IS NOT NULL`).

## Implementation notes
- New `GET /admin/api/v1/usage` endpoint, workspace-scoped.
- Filters: `capability`, `actor_user_id`, `status`, `since`.
- Payload per row: every column on `llm_usage` incl. the cd-wjpl
  additions.
- Index usage:
  - `ix_llm_usage_workspace_actor_created` for the actor filter.
  - `ix_llm_usage_workspace_capability_created` for the capability
    filter.
  - `ix_llm_usage_workspace_created` for the unfiltered feed.
- OpenAPI spec + a minimal /admin console page.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] The endpoint returns paginated `llm_usage` rows for the
      active workspace.
- [ ] Filters on `actor_user_id` hit the composite index.
- [ ] The payload surfaces all six cd-wjpl columns.
- [ ] Non-owner / non-manager principals receive 403.
- [ ] OpenAPI + CLI equivalents land together.

## Cross-cutting
- Downstream of cd-wjpl (columns + actor index) and cd-weue (real
  LLMClient, so there are rows to show).
- Blocks the admin-facing cost-tracking UX.

title: feat(llm): client wrapper that auto-invokes usage_recorder.record
description:
## Problem / goal
cd-wjpl shipped :func:`app.domain.llm.usage_recorder.record` as a
seam, not a decorator. Every LLM adapter caller still has to:

1. Call `check_budget` by hand before dispatch.
2. Catch transport / provider errors per-rung and advance the chain.
3. Measure latency, harvest token counts, call `record` after
   every attempt.

A client wrapper (§11 'Client abstraction') should close this over
so the caller passes capability + prompt and gets back an
`LLMResult` — the wrapper owns the fallback walk, the retry
policy, the latency stopwatch, and the budget + recorder
bookkeeping.

## Technical context
- Spec: `docs/specs/11-llm-and-agents.md` §'Client abstraction'
  (`LLMClient` Protocol; takes capability, emits `LLMResult` with
  `fallback_attempts`, `finish_reason`, `usage`).
- Seams already in place: :class:`~app.domain.llm.router.ModelPick`
  (resolver), :func:`check_budget` + :func:`record_usage` (budget),
  :func:`record` (post-flight telemetry), the OpenRouter adapter
  (:mod:`app.adapters.llm.openrouter`).

## Implementation notes
- New `app/domain/llm/client.py`:
  - Pure-async `class LLMClient` that composes router +
    adapter + budget + recorder.
  - Walks the ModelPick chain until a rung succeeds or the chain
    is exhausted.
  - Classifies errors (5xx, 429, timeout, transport, content
    refusal) into retryable vs terminal per §11 'Retryable errors'.
  - Propagates correlation_id through every attempt.
  - Emits one `LLMResult` on success or raises the last provider
    error on terminal failure.
- Adapter interface stays the same (the wrapper calls the adapter;
  the adapter doesn't know about budget or recorder).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] A caller passes `capability='chat.manager'` and gets an
      `LLMResult` with `usage`, `fallback_attempts`,
      `finish_reason` without touching budget / recorder by hand.
- [ ] Every attempted rung writes one `llm_usage` row
      (cd-wjpl recorder).
- [ ] `BudgetExceeded` propagates pre-flight with no row written.
- [ ] `X-LLM-Fallback-Attempts` response header echoes the final
      rung index.
- [ ] Unit tests: happy path, retryable-then-success, terminal
      error, budget-refused.

## Cross-cutting
- Downstream of cd-wjpl (uses the recorder) and cd-bnw (OpenRouter
  adapter).
- Lands before the first capability-using feature (`chat.manager`,
  `digest.manager`, …) goes through API.

title: feat(api): X-Correlation-Id / X-Correlation-Id-Echo middleware
description:
## Problem / goal
The cd-wjpl :func:`app.domain.llm.usage_recorder.record` carries
an inbound correlation id through the post-flight write and echoes
it via :class:`RecordedCall.correlation_id` — but there is no HTTP
middleware yet that reads `X-Correlation-Id` from the inbound
request (§11 'Client abstraction', §02 audit_log correlation scope)
and writes `X-Correlation-Id-Echo` onto the outbound response. The
recorder alone doesn't close the loop: callers would have to wire
the header plumbing by hand in every endpoint.

## Technical context
- Spec: `docs/specs/11-llm-and-agents.md` §'Client abstraction'
  (generate server-side if absent, return via `X-Correlation-Id-Echo`).
- Spec: `docs/specs/02-domain-model.md` §'Correlation scope'
  (defaults to the HTTP request id; caller may pass the same value
  across multiple requests to group them).
- Adjacent: `app.tenancy.middleware.WorkspaceContextMiddleware`
  already attaches `audit_correlation_id` to the WorkspaceContext;
  the HTTP header round-trip is the remaining piece.

## Implementation notes
- New `app/api/transport/correlation_id.py` middleware (FastAPI
  BaseHTTPMiddleware) reads `X-Correlation-Id`, generates a ULID
  if absent, stashes it on `request.state.correlation_id`, and
  writes `X-Correlation-Id-Echo` on the response.
- WorkspaceContextMiddleware reads `request.state.correlation_id`
  instead of minting its own.
- Any downstream code (audit writer, recorder, event bus) already
  reads the ctx's correlation id — no other changes needed.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Inbound `X-Correlation-Id` is preserved on every response's
      `X-Correlation-Id-Echo` header.
- [ ] Absent inbound header → server-minted ULID, echoed back.
- [ ] The same id lands on `audit_log.correlation_id`,
      `llm_usage.correlation_id`, and the outbound header.
- [ ] Integration test: one agent turn writes audit + llm_usage
      rows sharing the same id as the response header.

## Cross-cutting
- Downstream of cd-wjpl (the recorder already honours the id).
- Lands before the chat / embedded-agent UI consumes the echo
  header for its own correlation-trail view.

title: refactor(llm): rename llm_usage.model_id → provider_model_id
description:
## Problem / goal
The cd-cm5 slice landed the column llm_usage.model_id but the
§02 `llm_call` spec names the equivalent column provider_model_id
— the registry relation is a **provider-model** wire reference, not
the `llm_model` registry id. The cd-irng / cd-wjpl writers
populate it with the resolved provider-model wire name (taken from
:attr:`LlmUsage.api_model_id`), so the column body is already
correct; only the name is wrong.

## Why it was deferred in cd-wjpl
Renaming would churn every downstream reader in lockstep:

- /admin/usage feed queries (to land).
- cd-4btd registry wiring.
- The cd-irng budget sum in :func:`_sum_usage_cents`.
- Schema-fingerprint + inspect-based integration tests.

cd-wjpl documented the drift in :class:`LlmUsage`'s docstring and
on migration b8d0e1f2a3b4, but left the rename to a coordinated
follow-up.

## Implementation notes
- Alembic migration that renames the column (SQLite: batch_alter_
  table column-rename; PG: plain ALTER TABLE).
- Update :class:`app.adapters.db.llm.models.LlmUsage`.
- Update the cd-irng budget sum + /admin/usage queries.
- Update every integration test that asserts the column name
  (tests/integration/test_db_llm.py::TestMigrationShape,
  tests/unit/test_db_llm.py::TestLlmUsageModel).
- Remove the 'spec-drift note' from the model docstring once the
  column matches.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] llm_usage.provider_model_id exists on SQLite + PG.
- [ ] llm_usage.model_id removed on both shards.
- [ ] Every test in tests/domain/llm + tests/integration/llm still
      passes.
- [ ] Schema-fingerprint diff shows the rename only.
- [ ] Migration's downgrade() cleanly restores the old name.

## Cross-cutting
- Depends on: cd-4btd (llm_provider_model registry), /admin/usage
  endpoint landing first, so the readers and the column can flip
  together.

title: privacy/export: POST /api/v1/me/export with scope=export redaction
description:
## Problem

cd-a469 shipped `redact(payload, scope="export")` as a hook for the user-data export endpoint (§15 'Privacy and data rights'). The endpoint itself doesn't exist yet — only spec + mock. When we build it, the CSV / JSON serialiser must call `redact(..., scope="export")` on every free-text field so leaked PII (support-ticket bodies, task notes) is scrubbed before the bundle goes out.

## Scope
- `POST /api/v1/me/export` endpoint per §15.
- Queue a background job that builds a ZIP of the user's own data (profile, tasks, comments, bookings, expenses).
- Each free-text column in the bundle passes through `app.util.redact.redact(x, scope="export")`.
- Email delivery when ready (see §15 data-rights).

## Acceptance
- End-to-end test: user A requests export; the ZIP contains no PII belonging to other users, no credentials, no free-text emails / phones / IBANs.
- File delivery succeeds on SMTP mock.

## Related
- cd-a469 (parent redactor task)
- spec §15 'Privacy and data rights'

title: llm/redaction: load workspace ConsentSet from agent_preferences.upstream_pii_consent
description:
## Problem

cd-a469 shipped the PII redaction seam with a `ConsentSet` argument threaded through `OpenRouterClient.complete/chat/ocr/stream_chat`. Every call site currently passes `None` (→ `ConsentSet.none()`, redact-everything) because the DB column doesn't exist yet.

## Scope
- Add `agent_preferences.upstream_pii_consent` (jsonb) per spec §11 §'Redaction layer' / §'Consent'.
- Migration adds the column with empty default.
- Add a tiny loader `load_consent_set(session, workspace_id) -> ConsentSet`.
- Wire the LLM entry points (prompt builder, digest worker, agent router) to call the loader and pass the result into `OpenRouterClient`.
- Extend `tests/integration/llm/test_outbound_redaction.py` to cover the workspace-scoped toggle.

## Acceptance
- Workspace with `upstream_pii_consent: ["legal_name"]` preserves that field on outbound LLM calls.
- Workspace without consent scrubs everything — no regression on cd-a469's existing coverage.
- Owner manager UI exposes the toggle (out of scope if it's a separate task; file follow-up if so).

## Related
- cd-a469 (parent task)
- spec §11 §'Redaction layer'

title: llm/ocr: carve image-block base64 out of the redactor sweep
description:
## Problem

The §15 redactor (`app.util.redact.redact(scope="llm")`) runs on every OpenRouter outbound body. A vision/OCR request packages the image as a base64 data URL inside `messages[0].content[1].image_url.url`. The base64url credential rule (`\b[A-Za-z0-9_\-]{32,}\b`) matches the base64 payload and rewrites it with `<redacted:credential>`, so the provider never receives the image.

## Current state
- `tests/integration/llm/test_outbound_redaction.py::TestOcrRedaction::test_ocr_image_data_url_bytes_are_scrubbed_as_credential` pins the current (broken) behaviour so it's visible.
- The OCR capability (`google/gemma-3-27b-it`, §11) is therefore non-functional end-to-end today.

## Fix options
1. Exempt the image-block value from the regex scrub (skip `scrub_string` for keys named `image_url` under an `image` content block).
2. Wrap the image bytes in a typed sentinel that the walker recognises and passes through.
3. Redact the body at the `messages[*].content` level only, keeping binary/image blocks out of scope.

Option 3 is probably cleanest: the redactor never needed to walk image URLs in the first place. The LLM wire format for vision reliably separates text-content from image-content blocks.

## Acceptance
- `tests/integration/llm/test_outbound_redaction.py::TestOcrRedaction` asserts the data URL survives intact after redaction.
- An end-to-end OCR unit test (existing happy path) continues to pass.
- No regression on text-scrubbing for regular chat / complete calls.

## Related
- cd-a469 (original PII redactor task) landed the seam that causes this.
- spec §11 'Redaction layer' and §15 'Logging and redaction'.

title: feat(events): cross-worker event bus for SSE fanout under multi-worker deployment
description:
## Problem
`app.events.bus` is an **in-process** bus: `bus.publish(event)` only
reaches subscribers in the same Python process. The SSE transport
(`app.api.transport.sse.SSEFanOut`) subscribes on the same bus, so
SSE clients connected to worker A do NOT observe events published by
worker B.

Under today's single-worker dev stack (`docker compose … app-api`
runs one uvicorn process) this is invisible. Under a multi-worker
production deploy (`--workers N` or horizontal-scale behind a load
balancer) a notification published by the worker handling the write
reaches only the SSE clients connected to that same worker — a user
whose bell menu is served by a sibling worker sees no invalidation
until their next poll or manual reload.

Known affected events (the pattern is general but these are the
concrete user-visible hits):
- `notification.created` (cd-y1ge) — bell-menu unread miss
- `time.shift.changed` (cd-k0qf) — same pattern
- every `task.*` event when a manager is on one worker and a worker
  is on another

## Proposal
Drop a cross-worker relay in front of the in-process bus. Options:

- **Redis pub/sub.** Publisher writes to a Redis channel; every worker's
  bus subscribes to that channel and republishes locally. Cheap; one
  new infra dep (Redis already a likely v1 dep for session / cache).
- **Postgres `LISTEN/NOTIFY`.** Zero new infra (Postgres is already
  the primary store). Payload size cap (8KB NOTIFY limit) is not a
  practical issue for our event shapes. SQLite deployment still
  needs a different backend.
- **Database-polled outbox.** A table the service writes events to,
  a worker polls + republishes. Simplest; adds latency (poll interval).

Pick one per §01 "Deployment". Likely Redis if we are also using
it for session caching; Postgres LISTEN/NOTIFY if we are not.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Under `uvicorn --workers 2`, an event published on worker 1
      reaches SSE subscribers on worker 2.
- [ ] The in-process fast path (single-worker deploy, tests) is
      preserved.
- [ ] User-scoped events still filter per recipient on every worker.
- [ ] An integration test spawns two API processes and verifies
      cross-worker delivery for `notification.created` and
      `time.shift.changed`.

## Context
Tracked pattern: already called out in cd-k0qf (LLM router cache
invalidation) and cd-y1ge (notification fanout). This is the
umbrella task to actually fix it.

Discovered during cd-0kt8 (self-review of cd-y1ge). Spec: §01
"Deployment", §12 "REST API → SSE".

title: feat(messaging): persist email_delivery row on NotificationService mailer.send
description:
## Problem
Spec §10 specifies an `email_delivery` row per outbound email:

```
email_delivery
├── id
├── to_person_id
├── to_email_at_send           # snapshot
├── template_key
├── context_snapshot_json
├── sent_at
├── provider_message_id
├── delivery_state             # queued | sent | delivered | bounced | failed
├── first_error
├── retry_count
└── inbound_linkage            # reply-tracking, if any
```

The cd-y1ge `NotificationService` does NOT write this row. It calls
`mailer.send(...)` which returns a provider-assigned message id, and
then discards the id. The audit row (`messaging.notification.dispatched`
channel=email) is the only ledger entry.

This means:
- No query can answer "did this notification's email land?".
- Bounce / delivery webhooks have nothing to join against.
- A retry scheduler has no queue to walk.
- The `email_delivery` table defined in the schema is unused.

## Proposal
Wire `NotificationService` to persist an `email_delivery` row when
it calls `mailer.send(...)`:

1. Insert a `queued` row BEFORE the send.
2. On successful return, update `provider_message_id` and
   `delivery_state = 'sent'`.
3. On `MailDeliveryError`, update `delivery_state = 'failed'`,
   capture `first_error`.

Keep the audit row — audit is the cross-channel ledger; email_delivery
is the email-specific one. They complement each other.

## Open questions
- Does the retry scheduler live in the service (synchronous) or in
  a worker (pick up `queued` / `failed` rows)? Spec hints worker;
  the service should write `queued` and let the worker retry.
- Does `template_key` carry the notification kind ("task_assigned")
  or the template filename ("task_assigned.subject.j2")? The
  column name suggests the former.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Every mailer.send from NotificationService results in one
      email_delivery row.
- [ ] Row carries to_email_at_send, template_key, context_snapshot_json.
- [ ] delivery_state transitions queued → sent (happy path) or
      queued → failed on MailDeliveryError.
- [ ] Bounce / delivered webhooks can locate the row by
      provider_message_id.
- [ ] Existing email opt-out audit reason is preserved (opted-out
      emails never reach the mailer, so no email_delivery row).
- [ ] Unit + integration tests cover both the send and
      MailDeliveryError branches.

## Context
Discovered during cd-0kt8 (self-review of cd-y1ge).
- Spec: docs/specs/10-messaging-notifications.md §"Delivery tracking".
- DB model landed with cd-aqwt (email_delivery table exists; the
  service just doesn't populate it).

title: spec-drift(messaging): reconcile NotificationKind vocabulary with §10 "Emails the system sends" table
description:
## Problem
Spec §10 "Emails the system sends" lists 18 distinct kinds of
outbound email: magic link, daily owner/manager digest, daily worker
digest, task overdue alert, task comment mention, issue reported,
expense submitted, expense decision, payslip issued, iCal feed error,
anomaly detected, availability override pending, pre-arrival task
unassigned, task primary unavailable, holiday schedule impact, agent
approval pending, invoice reminder, invoice reminder exhausted,
property_workspace invite.

The DB CHECK (`_NOTIFICATION_KIND_VALUES` in
`app.adapters.db.messaging.models`) enumerates 14: task_assigned,
task_overdue, expense_approved, expense_rejected, expense_submitted,
approval_needed, approval_decided, issue_reported, issue_resolved,
comment_mention, payslip_issued, stay_upcoming, anomaly_detected,
agent_message.

`NotificationKind` in `app.domain.messaging.notifications` mirrors
the DB CHECK one-for-one (an import-time guard refuses to import
under drift).

## Drift
Listed in §10, NOT in the CHECK:
- magic_link (handled by auth directly, not via NotificationService)
- daily digest (owner / manager / worker variants) — digest_record
  table owns this
- ical_feed_error
- availability_override_pending
- pre_arrival_task_unassigned
- task_primary_unavailable
- holiday_schedule_impact
- agent_approval_pending (today it is "approval_needed"?)
- invoice_reminder
- invoice_reminder_exhausted
- property_workspace_invite

In the CHECK / enum, NOT in §10:
- task_assigned
- expense_approved / expense_rejected (§10 has "expense decision")
- issue_resolved
- stay_upcoming
- approval_decided
- agent_message

## Options
- **A. Widen the enum + CHECK** to cover every §10 kind that flows
  through NotificationService. Auth keeps its own direct-mail path
  (magic_link stays out of the enum). Digests own the digest_record
  taxonomy; they do not land in `notification`. Every other §10
  row becomes a `NotificationKind` value + a template trio
  (`<kind>.subject.j2` / `<kind>.body_md.j2` / optional push).

- **B. Update §10** to name the canonical enum and reroute the
  prose to match the code. Kind names in the spec switch to the
  snake_case values ("task_assigned" not "task assignment"). A
  single table maps the §10 English labels to the enum.

Option B is cheaper and probably correct: the spec language is a
user-facing taxonomy, the enum is the developer-facing one, they
should mutually reference rather than duplicate.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] §10 and `NotificationKind` enumerate the same set of kinds OR
      the spec names explicitly which §10 rows land via
      NotificationService vs other paths (auth mailer, digest worker).
- [ ] The §10 / spec / enum alignment is verified by a test that
      reads the spec markdown table and asserts coverage.
- [ ] The existing import-time guard
      (`NotificationKind enum / DB CHECK`) stays green.

## Context
Discovered during cd-0kt8 (self-review of cd-y1ge).
- Spec: docs/specs/10-messaging-notifications.md §"Emails the
  system sends".
- Code: app/domain/messaging/notifications.py (NotificationKind);
  app/adapters/db/messaging/models.py (_NOTIFICATION_KIND_VALUES).

title: spec-drift(messaging): unify template directory layout — app/templates/email, app/mail/templates, app/domain/messaging/templates
description:
## Problem
Three template locations coexist in the tree today:

1. `app/templates/email/` — the location spec §10 pins (Jinja2 + MJML,
   non-existent on disk as of cd-y1ge).
2. `app/mail/templates/*.py` — used by the auth mailer (plain Python
   `str.format_map`, concrete files: magic_link.py, invite_accept.py,
   recovery_*).
3. `app/domain/messaging/templates/*.j2` — used by the notification
   service (cd-y1ge), Jinja2 file-resident.

Two formats (Python format_map vs Jinja2), three locations, and one
spec pin that currently points at an empty path. A next agent
adding a template will land on (1), (2), or (3) more-or-less at
random and the divergence will grow.

## Why it matters
- A future translator / i18n pass has to touch every file in every
  tree instead of walking one root.
- Operators browsing `app/` for "how do I change the email copy"
  get three wrong hits depending on which subtree they open first.
- Spec §10 now lies ("Jinja2 templates under `app/templates/email/`").
- Cross-context DRY is impossible: the auth mailer and the
  notification service both render a "Hi {{ display_name }}, click
  this link" body but via different engines.

## Proposal
Pick ONE location + ONE engine. Candidates:

- **A. `app/templates/email/` (spec-aligned, Jinja2).** Move auth +
  notification templates under this root. Keep the locale fallback
  convention the notification service already uses. Auth mailer
  switches from `str.format_map` → Jinja2 (trivial: the current
  bodies are one-variable interpolations).

- **B. `app/domain/messaging/templates/` (service-aligned).** Update
  the spec to name this path as the canonical tree; move auth
  templates here; drop the `app/templates/email/` pin in §10.

Either is fine; the decision is whether the spec follows the code
or the code follows the spec. Defer the call to the next
maintainer — this task is the coordination point, not the
implementation.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] One template location on disk, named in §10.
- [ ] Auth mailer and notification service share one engine.
- [ ] Spec §10 §"Template system" mentions the chosen path once.
- [ ] No dead directory remains.
- [ ] Existing tests still pass; migration is a mechanical move +
      minimum syntax swap on the auth side.

## Context
- Surface spec: docs/specs/10-messaging-notifications.md §"Template
  system".
- cd-y1ge implementation: app/domain/messaging/notifications.py,
  app/domain/messaging/templates/.
- Auth mailer: app/mail/templates/*.py.

Discovered during cd-0kt8 (self-review of cd-y1ge).

title: feat(web/sse): dispatcher handler for llm.assignment.changed (admin LlmPage cache invalidation)
description:
## Problem / goal

cd-k0qf added the workspace-scope SSE event
``llm.assignment.changed`` (fires on any mutation to
``model_assignment`` or ``llm_capability_inheritance`` in a
workspace; restricted to ``manager`` role; see
``app/events/types.py:173``). The backend cache-invalidation
path is live; the frontend EventSource dispatcher
(``mocks/web/src/lib/sse.ts``, future ``app/web/src/lib/sse.ts``)
does not yet subscribe to it.

Once cd-ko40 (LlmPage admin UI) adds its TanStack Query keys,
the SSE dispatcher must invalidate those keys on
``llm.assignment.changed`` so a second admin tab / device sees
the chain update without a page reload — per §14
"SSE-driven invalidation".

## Technical context

- Event definition: ``app/events/types.py:173``
- Router cache invalidator: ``app/domain/llm/router.py:249``
- Frontend dispatcher (mocks): ``mocks/web/src/lib/sse.ts:80``
  (the ``events`` array that ``addEventListener`` walks).
- Frontend dispatcher (prod shell): ``app/web/src/lib/sse.ts``
- LlmPage page: ``mocks/web/src/pages/admin/LlmPage.tsx``
  (tracked under cd-ko40).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] ``llm.assignment.changed`` is listed in the
      ``events: SseEvent["event"][]`` array in both mocks and
      prod frontend dispatchers.
- [ ] ``dispatch()`` handler invalidates the LlmPage query keys
      (``qk.adminLlmGraph()`` or equivalent — define alongside
      cd-ko40).
- [ ] Frontend vitest covers the new case: a fake SSE payload
      triggers ``invalidateQueries`` on the expected key.

## Out of scope

- Backend (already live).
- Capability-scoped invalidation — whole-workspace is sufficient
  for v1 per the router's own caching strategy.

title: docs(spec/events): document workspace-scope llm.assignment.changed SSE event in §11 taxonomy
description:
## Problem / goal

cd-k0qf registered a new workspace-scope SSE event
``llm.assignment.changed`` (see ``app/events/types.py:173``) on
``/w/<slug>/events`` that the §11 router uses for cache
invalidation. ``docs/specs/12-rest-api.md`` names the
**deployment**-scope sibling ``admin.llm.assignment_updated`` on
``/admin/events`` (§12 "SSE"), but the workspace-scope event is
not listed anywhere in the specs.

``docs/specs/11-llm-and-agents.md`` should grow a short event-
taxonomy entry under §"Model assignment" (or a new §"SSE events")
that names ``llm.assignment.changed``, pins its
``allowed_roles = ("manager",)``, and states the contract: "fires
on any mutation to ``model_assignment`` or
``llm_capability_inheritance`` in a workspace; admin / manager
UIs invalidate their LlmPage queries; the §11 router drops its
per-workspace resolver cache".

For symmetry, ``docs/specs/12-rest-api.md`` §"SSE" should cross-
reference the §11 taxonomy so a reader landing on either spec
finds both events in one hop.

## Technical context

- Event: ``app/events/types.py:173 LlmAssignmentChanged``
- Router cache subscriber: ``app/domain/llm/router.py:249``
- Spec §12 existing deployment-scope event:
  ``docs/specs/12-rest-api.md:638``
- Frontend SSE dispatcher that will subscribe once LlmPage lands:
  ``mocks/web/src/lib/sse.ts`` (under cd-ko40)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] §11 documents ``llm.assignment.changed``: scope (workspace),
      ``allowed_roles`` tuple, payload fields, and the two
      consumers (router cache, LlmPage UI).
- [ ] §12 "SSE" section cross-references §11.
- [ ] ``audit-spec`` run clean after the edit.

## Out of scope

- Implementation changes (the event already ships).
- Richer payload (capability-scoped invalidation) — tracked
  separately if/when the UI needs it.

title: spec-drift(llm): reconcile table-name llm_assignment vs model_assignment
description:
## Problem

The spec uses two different names for the same table:

- `docs/specs/11-llm-and-agents.md` §"Model assignment" line ~828 pins `llm_assignment` (with `UNIQUE(capability, priority)`).
- `docs/specs/02-domain-model.md` line 301 lists both `model_assignment` and `llm_assignment` as entity names.
- `docs/specs/11-llm-and-agents.md` line ~430, ~646, ~796, ~1098 reference `llm_assignment`.
- `docs/specs/20-glossary.md` line 725 references `llm_assignment`.

The **code** (cd-cm5 + cd-u84y) uses `model_assignment`:
- `app/adapters/db/llm/models.py::ModelAssignment.__tablename__ = "model_assignment"`
- `migrations/versions/…_e5f6a7b8c9d0_llm_cd_cm5.py` creates `model_assignment`.

## What needs to happen

Pick one canonical name and sweep the other:

**Option A — rename table to `llm_assignment`** (matches §11's detailed schema block, matches the fj2 prior art cited in the spec). Implementation cost: one migration that renames `model_assignment` → `llm_assignment`, rewrites the class, rewires every reference (docstrings, tests, mocks). The spec's intent appears to be `llm_assignment` (every schema diagram uses it; `model_assignment` only appears once, in the §02 entity list alongside `llm_assignment` as an alias).

**Option B — keep `model_assignment` and strip `llm_assignment`** from every spec reference. Cheaper, but loses the `llm_*` naming symmetry (`llm_provider`, `llm_model`, `llm_provider_model`, `llm_call`, `llm_usage_daily`, `llm_capability_inheritance`, `llm_prompt_template`, `llm_prompt_template_revision` — all `llm_*`).

Spec authors' intent should break the tie. Bias toward Option A if anything.

## Context

- Pre-existing drift — not introduced by cd-u84y; cd-u84y merely extended the existing `model_assignment` table (same name as cd-cm5 picked).
- If Option A lands, it should land before cd-k0qf (resolver) so the resolver's code references the final name.
- Neither self-review iteration can unilaterally rewrite this — needs spec-author sign-off.

title: Self-review: chore(web/styles): migrate raw-pixel spacing in mock CSS to --space-N tokens
description:
## Problem / goal
Auto-fixing self-review of the changes made under cd-1mxw. Catch bugs,
missing pieces, and unintended consequences before they ship.

**Depends on: cd-1mxw** (main task must be complete first).

## How to run
Run \`/selfreview\` in **autofix mode** against the commit(s) from cd-1mxw.

- Do NOT enter plan mode.
- Do NOT ask the user to triage findings — you are the triage.
- Apply fixes for every BUGS, MISSING, and RISKY finding.
- Skip NITPICKS unless trivially safe.
- Run the quality gates after fixing.
- Commit the fixes, push, and close this task.

See [\`.claude/skills/selfreview/SKILL.md\`](.claude/skills/selfreview/SKILL.md).

## Specific things to verify
- No surface visually shifted by 1-2 px due to mis-mapped tokens
  (e.g. someone replaced 18px with var(--space-9) [20px] without
  noting it as an intentional snap)
- Justified exceptions actually have a justifying comment, not a TODO
- No raw pixel \`padding\`/\`margin\`/\`gap\` slipped through under
  \`@media\` blocks or pseudo-selectors (the verification grep is line-
  oriented, so multi-line declarations or unusual indentation can
  evade it — re-grep without the \`^\s+\` anchor as a cross-check)
- Light/dark parity: dark mode spacing matches light mode (since
  spacing tokens live on \`:root\` only)

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] All BUGS from the self-review fixed
- [ ] All MISSING pieces completed
- [ ] All RISKY items mitigated (or justified in a task comment)
- [ ] Linter, formatter, type checker, affected tests all pass
- [ ] Fixes committed and pushed

title: chore(web/styles): migrate raw-pixel spacing in mock CSS to --space-N tokens
description:
## Problem / goal

DESIGN.md standardized the spacing scale to
\`0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 20, 24, 32, 40, 48, 56\` px,
but the mock CSS uses raw pixel values throughout — including many
that aren't on the new scale (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15, 18, 22, 28,
30, 36, 44, 52). Replace raw pixel \`padding\`, \`margin\`, and \`gap\`
values with \`var(--space-N)\` references; snap off-scale values to
the nearest scale value or justify them with a one-line comment.

This is what makes the scale enforceable: once the tokens are in,
diffs that introduce raw pixel spacing become visible in review.

**Depends on cd-3qy2** (tokens must exist first).

## Technical context

- **Primary file**: \`mocks/web/src/styles/globals.css\` (~2200 lines,
  hundreds of raw pixel uses)
- **Other CSS**: \`rg --files mocks/web/src -g "*.css"\` to find any
  others (currently only reset.css, globals.css, tokens.css)
- **Source of truth**: \`mocks/web/src/styles/tokens.css\` after cd-3qy2
- **Scale mapping** (px → token):
  - 0 → \`var(--space-0)\`
  - 2 → \`var(--space-1)\`
  - 4 → \`var(--space-2)\`
  - 6 → \`var(--space-3)\`
  - 8 → \`var(--space-4)\`
  - 10 → \`var(--space-5)\`
  - 12 → \`var(--space-6)\`
  - 14 → \`var(--space-7)\`
  - 16 → \`var(--space-8)\`
  - 20 → \`var(--space-9)\`
  - 24 → \`var(--space-10)\`
  - 32 → \`var(--space-11)\`
  - 40 → \`var(--space-12)\`
  - 48 → \`var(--space-13)\`
  - 56 → \`var(--space-14)\`

## Implementation notes

- Apply only to \`padding\`, \`padding-*\`, \`margin\`, \`margin-*\`,
  \`gap\`, \`row-gap\`, \`column-gap\` — NOT to \`width\`, \`height\`,
  \`top/left/right/bottom\`, \`border-*\`, \`font-size\`, \`line-height\`
- Off-scale values: snap to nearest scale value by default. Keep the
  raw value only if the visual delta matters (e.g. \`padding-bottom: 132px\`
  to clear a fixed sticky bar — that's a measured offset, not a scale
  value). When you keep one, add a one-line CSS comment justifying it,
  e.g. \`padding-bottom: 132px; /* matches sticky CTA bar height */\`
- \`env(safe-area-inset-*)\` expressions stay as-is — they're platform
  values, not scale values
- Composite shorthand (\`padding: 12px 16px\`) → \`padding: var(--space-6) var(--space-8)\`
- Calc expressions: prefer \`calc(var(--space-9) + env(safe-area-inset-bottom))\`
  over raw \`calc(20px + env(...))\`
- Don't touch \`-moz-\`/\`-webkit-\` prefix-only declarations or anything
  inside \`@supports\` blocks unless trivial
- Take it module-by-module (cards, panels, navigation, layout,
  forms, etc.) so a regression is bisectable
- Run the dev stack and visually verify each major surface after
  each batch — type-checks alone won't catch a misapplied token

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] No raw \`px\` values remain for \`padding\`, \`margin\`, or \`gap\`
      in \`mocks/web/src/styles/globals.css\` except those with an
      adjacent justifying comment OR inside \`env()\`/\`calc()\` of a
      platform value
- [ ] Verification command returns only justified exceptions:
      \`rg "^\s+(padding|margin|gap)[^:]*:\s*[0-9]" mocks/web/src/styles/globals.css | rg -v "/\*"\`
- [ ] No visual regression vs. main on the worker home, manager
      dashboard, settings page, and any modal — verified manually
      in the dev stack at 360px, 720px, 1080px viewports
- [ ] Vite build passes: \`docker compose -f mocks/docker-compose.yml exec mocks-web pnpm build\`
- [ ] Playwright screenshots of the four key surfaces saved under
      \`.playwright-mcp/\` for the PR

## Test plan

### Automated
\`\`\`bash
# Find any remaining raw pixel spacing (should match only justified exceptions)
rg "^\s+(padding|margin|gap)[^:]*:\s*[0-9]" mocks/web/src/styles/globals.css \\
  | rg -v "/\*"
# Expect: empty, or only env()/calc() platform values

# Build still passes
docker compose -f mocks/docker-compose.yml exec mocks-web pnpm build

# CSS still parses cleanly
docker compose -f mocks/docker-compose.yml exec mocks-web pnpm lint
\`\`\`

### Manual
1. \`docker compose -f mocks/docker-compose.yml up -d --build\`
2. Visit http://127.0.0.1:8100 — sign in via dev login script
3. For each viewport (360, 720, 1080):
   - Worker home (\`/w/<slug>\`)
   - Manager dashboard (\`/w/<slug>/manage\`)
   - Settings page
   - One modal (e.g. task-detail)
   - Compare with screenshots from main; flag any visual diff
4. Toggle light/dark mode — confirm spacing unchanged

title: feat(web/styles): add spacing tokens (--space-0 … --space-14) to tokens.css
description:
## Problem / goal

DESIGN.md just standardized the spacing scale to
`0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 20, 24, 32, 40, 48, 56` px
(see DESIGN.md:289-292 prose and DESIGN.md:92-106 frontmatter).

`mocks/web/src/styles/tokens.css` has zero spacing custom properties —
only colors and shadows. The absence of tokens is the root cause of
the drift this change fixed: with nothing to reach for, every component
freelances raw pixel values (everything from 1px to 56px in 1px steps).

Add `--space-N` custom properties so future component CSS can reference
them and the scale is enforced by convention.

## Technical context

- **Key file**: `mocks/web/src/styles/tokens.css`
- **Source of truth**: DESIGN.md frontmatter under `spacing:` (lines 92-106)
- **Pattern**: tokens are exposed as `:root` custom properties; dark
  mode is a manual `[data-theme="dark"]` override
- **Important**: spacing is theme-agnostic. Define on `:root, [data-theme="light"]`
  ONLY — do NOT duplicate under `[data-theme="dark"]`

## Implementation notes

- Use the same numbering as DESIGN.md frontmatter:
  - `--space-0: 0` (or `0px` for consistency with other px values)
  - `--space-1: 2px`
  - `--space-2: 4px`
  - `--space-3: 6px`
  - `--space-4: 8px`
  - `--space-5: 10px`
  - `--space-6: 12px`
  - `--space-7: 14px`
  - `--space-8: 16px`
  - `--space-9: 20px`
  - `--space-10: 24px`
  - `--space-11: 32px`
  - `--space-12: 40px`
  - `--space-13: 48px`
  - `--space-14: 56px`
- Group under a comment block so the intent is clear
- Do NOT modify globals.css in this task — migration is a separate
  task (blocked by this one)

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] All 15 `--space-N` tokens defined on `:root, [data-theme="light"]`
      in `mocks/web/src/styles/tokens.css`
- [ ] No duplication under `[data-theme="dark"]` block
- [ ] Each token value matches the corresponding DESIGN.md frontmatter
      entry exactly
- [ ] `rg "^\s+--space-" mocks/web/src/styles/tokens.css | wc -l` returns 15

## Test plan

### Automated
\`\`\`bash
# Verify all tokens exist
rg "^\s+--space-(0|[1-9]|1[0-4]):" mocks/web/src/styles/tokens.css | wc -l
# Expect: 15

# Verify no dark-mode override
sed -n '/data-theme="dark"/,/^}/p' mocks/web/src/styles/tokens.css | rg "space"
# Expect: empty

# Vite build still succeeds
docker compose -f mocks/docker-compose.yml exec mocks-web pnpm build
\`\`\`

### Manual
1. \`docker compose -f mocks/docker-compose.yml up -d --build\`
2. Open http://127.0.0.1:8100 and toggle dark/light mode
3. Inspect a card in DevTools — confirm \`--space-N\` properties are
   visible under \`:root\` computed styles
4. Confirm no visual regression (this task adds tokens but doesn't
   consume them)

title: feat(db/authz): role_grant soft-retire columns (revoked_at, started_on, ended_on)
description:
## Problem / goal
§02 role_grants defines a richer schema than the v1 slice ships: revoked_at, started_on, ended_on, revoked_by_user_id. Today's revoke is a hard DELETE (see app/domain/identity/role_grants.py::revoke docstring), which makes 'non-archived grants' == 'row exists' — fine for v1 but wrong for any flow that wants to keep the audit trail of a revoked grant while still excluding it from live checks.

Self-service recovery kill-switch (cd-jdhm) lands today with that v1 assumption. app.auth.recovery.is_self_service_recovery_disabled walks every extant role_grant for the user; when revoked_at arrives, its WHERE clause extends with RoleGrant.revoked_at IS NULL. The helper's public contract stays unchanged.

## Technical context
- Spec: docs/specs/02-domain-model.md §'role_grants'.
- Current model: app/adapters/db/authz/models.py (RoleGrant).
- Current domain: app/domain/identity/role_grants.py — revoke() currently DELETEs.
- Integration test shim: tests/integration/auth/test_recovery_full_flow.py::TestRecoveryKillSwitch::test_archived_grants_do_not_block uses 'archive == delete' today; must stay green after the soft-retire migration.

## Implementation notes
- New Alembic migration adds revoked_at (tstz?), started_on (date?), ended_on (date?), revoked_by_user_id (FK user.id).
- Partial unique index (revoked_at IS NULL) on (workspace_id, user_id, grant_role, scope_property_id) — §02 'role_grants Invariants'.
- Update role_grants.revoke() to set revoked_at instead of DELETE; keep the last-owner guard.
- Audit row shape unchanged.
- Update app.auth.recovery.is_self_service_recovery_disabled WHERE clause to filter revoked_at IS NULL.
- Update membership / permission resolver helpers that walk role_grant to also filter revoked_at IS NULL.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Migration adds the four columns + partial unique index on both SQLite + Postgres
- [ ] role_grants.revoke() soft-retires; hard-delete paths are purged
- [ ] is_self_service_recovery_disabled skips revoked grants (new test case)
- [ ] All existing role_grant callers updated — full test suite green
- [ ] docs/specs/02 updated if any shape drifted during implementation

title: feat(web/tokens): UI for delegated + personal access tokens
description: cd-i1qe added the HTTP surfaces (POST /w/<slug>/api/v1/auth/tokens with delegate:true; POST /api/v1/me/tokens + GET + DELETE) but no frontend. §14 'Tokens' panel should gain a 'Delegated' option on the workspace /tokens page and a new 'Personal access tokens' panel on /me/security. Load /frontend-design:frontend-design before writing React. Acceptance: manager can mint a delegated token from /w/<slug>/tokens and the row is clearly flagged; any user can mint a PAT with me:* scopes from /me and revoke it; 'shown only once' copy surfaces the plaintext per §03.

title: fix(logging): RedactionFilter._JWT_RE swallows dotted event markers (observability bug)
description:
## Problem
`app/util/logging.py:_JWT_RE` is `\\b[A-Za-z0-9_\\-]+\\.[A-Za-z0-9_\\-]+\\.[A-Za-z0-9_\\-]+\\b` — three base64url-shaped segments separated by dots. Every three-dot event marker the app emits matches this pattern and is replaced with `***REDACTED***` before the JSON line reaches stdout.

Verified against the current worker bootstrap (cd-7c0p) but the regression is pre-existing:

Redacted today:
- `worker.scheduler.started` / `stopped` / `start_noop` / `stop_noop`
- `worker.tick.start` / `end` / `error`
- `worker.lifespan.started` / `skipped`
- `worker.process.started` / `stopping` / `stopped` / `crashed`
- `worker.signal.unsupported`
- `worker.generator.placeholder`
- `worker.heartbeat.error`
- `ops.readyz.degraded` / `db_error` / `migrations_read_error` / `script_tree_error`

Not redacted (two dots):
- `worker.mode`

Operational impact: every existing operator-facing `event=` marker becomes `event="***REDACTED***"` in the log stream. Dashboards / alerting that filter on `event=worker.tick.error` see nothing. Incident response loses the primary grep target.

## Why this wasn't caught sooner
Pre-existing — the filter was written to catch raw JWTs pasted into log messages, and the regex is correctly permissive for arbitrary bearer strings. The exact-`event`-field case is new traffic from the worker bootstrap. There's no test for the reverse — "plain dotted identifier survives redaction."

## Fix direction
Options (in order of preference):
1. **Tighten `_JWT_RE`.** Require at least one segment to have length ≥ 10 (real JWT segments are huge). Plain `worker.scheduler.started` has segments 6/9/7 — below any realistic threshold — and `eyJhbG.abc.def` has a 6-char first segment. The minimum-length gate must therefore apply to at least one segment, not all three. Something like `\\b[\\w-]{10,}\\.[\\w-]+\\.[\\w-]+\\b|\\b[\\w-]+\\.[\\w-]{10,}\\.[\\w-]+\\b|\\b[\\w-]+\\.[\\w-]+\\.[\\w-]{10,}\\b` — exhaustive and still under-redacts only clearly non-JWT strings.
2. **Exempt the `event` extra key entirely.** The field is always an app-authored literal, never user-supplied.
3. **Do both.** Belt-and-braces.

## Acceptance
- [ ] `RedactionFilter` no longer masks `worker.*` / `ops.*` / any lowercase three-segment dotted identifier under 10 chars per segment.
- [ ] Existing JWT test (`tests/unit/test_logging.py::test_jwt_shape_in_message` with `eyJhbG.abc.def`) keeps failing — i.e. still redacted. Pick a realistic test vector (full header.payload.signature) if the current one is too short for the new rule.
- [ ] New tests cover at least: `worker.scheduler.started`, `ops.readyz.degraded`, `a.b.c` (all survive); realistic JWT (redacted).

## Why filed now
Self-review of cd-7c0p (`/selfreview` autofix mode) surfaced this. Fixing is out of scope for that task — `app/util/logging.py` is owned by a separate set of specs (§15 "Logging and redaction"). Filing so operators don't lose visibility into the brand-new worker event stream.

title: fix(worker): UPSERT race on worker_heartbeat when >1 scheduler shares a DB
description:
## Problem
`app/worker/heartbeat.py::upsert_heartbeat` is a SELECT-then-INSERT/UPDATE pattern with no `ON CONFLICT` clause. Today the single-process AsyncIOScheduler (internal mode) or single `worker` container (external mode) makes the window between SELECT and INSERT "effectively zero", but the Recipe D self-hosted-SaaS shape documented in `docs/specs/16-deployment-operations.md` permits horizontal scale-out of the worker container. Two schedulers in different processes racing the first-ever write of `worker_name='scheduler_heartbeat'` both SELECT nothing, both INSERT, and one loses on the `UniqueConstraint(worker_name)` backstop — surfacing as `IntegrityError`, dropping that tick's heartbeat on the floor, and potentially escalating `/readyz` red unnecessarily.

A second live-config path to the same race: a misconfigured deployment that accidentally runs BOTH `CREWDAY_WORKER=internal` in the web container AND a separate `worker` container. Spec §01 describes this as "two ticks per cron boundary" (a compute waste) but the heartbeat upsert is the first surface where it becomes a correctness problem.

## Scope
- Replace the SELECT-then-INSERT/UPDATE in `upsert_heartbeat` with a portable UPSERT:
  - SQLite: `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT(worker_name) DO UPDATE SET heartbeat_at = excluded.heartbeat_at`.
  - Postgres: `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT ON CONSTRAINT ... DO UPDATE` (use the same `excluded` alias).
  - SQLAlchemy 2.x: `sqlalchemy.dialects.sqlite.insert` / `sqlalchemy.dialects.postgresql.insert` with a branch on `session.bind.dialect.name` — pattern is already used elsewhere in `app/adapters/db/` (grep for `.on_conflict_do_update`).

## Acceptance
- [ ] `upsert_heartbeat` is one `INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE` statement; no SELECT-then-branch.
- [ ] Unit test: two sessions both call `upsert_heartbeat` with the same `worker_name` inside one transaction; the second call updates the same row without `IntegrityError`.
- [ ] Unit test: matrix on sqlite and postgres dialects (or at least dialect-stubbed statement generation).
- [ ] `tests/integration/test_worker_scheduler.py` still green.

## Why filed now
Self-review of cd-7c0p surfaced this. The code lands today because single-process is the current reality; the fix is cheap once `>1` scheduler is actually on the roadmap. Filing so the debt is visible against Recipe D plans.

title: chore(worker): job-failure alerting + metrics surface
description:
## Problem / goal
cd-7c0p's ``wrap_job`` swallows every ``Exception`` with an ERROR log. Downstream, the only signal is ``/readyz`` going red via heartbeat staleness — no per-job counter, no alerting seam.

## Scope
- Emit a Prometheus counter per ``(job_id, outcome)`` alongside the log (§16 "Metrics" — ``worker-job durations`` already hints at this).
- Write a structured audit record (:func:`app.audit.write_audit`) on repeated failures (threshold e.g. 3 ticks in a row) so managers see it in the activity feed.
- A killswitch: mark a job as ``dead`` after N consecutive failures so the scheduler stops firing it until a manual reset.

## Acceptance criteria
- Metric appears in ``/metrics`` scrape with the job_id label.
- Repeated-failure audit row lands; reset via CLI.
- Unit tests for the counter + killswitch.

## Depends
- cd-7c0p.

title: design: clarify /admin/signups scope — workspace-operator vs deployment-operator (§15 vs cd-g1ay)
description:
## Problem / goal
Spec §15 "Self-serve abuse mitigations" describes `/admin/signups` as the "operator-only" page for surfaced signup abuse signals (burst-rate trips, same-IP-many-emails, repeat provisioning, quota near-breach). Throughout §15, "operator" consistently means **deployment operator** (the person running the deployment, bare-host `/admin/*` shell per §14). cd-g1ay implemented this as a **workspace-scoped** surface at `/w/<slug>/api/v1/admin/signups` per the tasks explicit "workspace-scoped" wording.

The two readings diverge:

- **Pre-workspace signals** (burst-rate on `POST /signup/start`, same IP across distinct *emails*, repeat email) have no workspace attached — they happen before a workspace exists. A workspace-scoped page cannot show them.
- **Post-workspace signals** (quota near-breach on unverified workspaces) do have a workspace and a workspace owner/manager could reasonably want to see them.

cd-g1ay landed the workspace-scoped scaffold as a placeholder (empty `data: []`). cd-ovt4 will wire the real query, so this decision must be resolved before cd-ovt4 starts.

## Options
1. **Keep workspace-scoped** (status quo after cd-g1ay). The page shows only `quota_near_breach` events for the callers own workspace. Pre-workspace signals are invisible here — they live on a separate deployment-admin page (TBD, probably `/admin/api/v1/signups` on the bare host).
2. **Move to deployment-scoped**. The page lives at `/admin/api/v1/signups` and shows every signup abuse signal the deployment has seen, cross-workspace. Workspace owners never see it. This matches §15s "operator" wording most directly.
3. **Dual surface**. Workspace-scoped for "my workspaces quota trips"; deployment-scoped for "global signup abuse". Two endpoints, two pages, two CLI groups.

## Why it matters now
- cd-ovt4 cannot write the query without knowing the scope.
- The mock SPA SignupsPage.tsx currently lives in ManagerLayout, implying workspace-scoped; moving it to deployment scope means rewriting the layout wiring.
- The `audit.signup.suspicious` row schema cd-ovt4 writes must carry or omit `workspace_id` based on the decision.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Spec §15 clarifies which surface owns `/admin/signups`
- [ ] cd-g1ays router either stays at `/w/<slug>/api/v1/admin/signups` or is moved to `/admin/api/v1/signups` (deployment tree)
- [ ] cd-ovt4 updated to match the chosen scope
- [ ] SPA routing and cd-wzk0 (§13/§14 doc edits) reflect the final answer

## Blocks
- cd-ovt4 (suspicious-activity surfacing) — should not start until this is decided

title: docs(spec): document workspace-admin CLI group + SPA /signups route in §13, §14
description:
## Problem / goal
cd-g1ay landed the workspace-scoped admin aggregator at `/w/<slug>/api/v1/admin/*` with OpenAPI tag `workspace_admin` and CLI group `workspace-admin`. The selfreview (cd-ugea) confirmed the naming avoids the §13 reserved `crewday admin` (host-CLI-only) and `crewday deploy` (deployment-HTTP) groups, but §13 does not yet document `workspace-admin` as a third CLI group, and §14 `/w/<slug>/` owner/manager route list does not list the `/signups` (or `/admin/signups`) page.

## Scope
- `docs/specs/13-cli.md` — add a short subsection under "crewday admin vs crewday deploy" introducing `crewday workspace-admin` as the workspace-scoped HTTP admin group. First verb will be `crewday workspace-admin signups-list`.
- `docs/specs/14-web-frontend.md` — add `signups` (or the final URL chosen at integration time) to the "Owner/Manager (under /w/<slug>/)" route list around line 187. Cross-ref §15 "Self-serve abuse mitigations".
- Consider whether `operation_id` prefix `workspace_admin.*` (underscore) vs `x-cli.group` `workspace-admin` (dash) should be harmonised — the split mirrors §12 operation-id-is-dotted-and-underscored vs §13 CLI-groups-are-dashed convention, but a spec note avoids future confusion.

## Why not in cd-g1ay
cd-g1ay was scaffold-only. Spec §13/§14 edits are out of scope for the router scaffold and belong in a focused docs PR.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] §13 names `workspace-admin` as a first-class CLI group with scope = workspace HTTP admin
- [ ] §14 lists the signups page under the owner/manager routes
- [ ] `/update-openapi` re-run (no schema change expected) confirms spec matches docs

## Blocked by
- None — docs-only follow-up. Safe to land any time after cd-g1ay.

title: feat(authz): introduce admin.view action_key for workspace-admin surfaces
description:
## Problem / goal
The workspace-scoped `/admin/*` router (cd-g1ay) gates on `audit_log.view` today — the closest existing catalog entry. As more workspace-admin surfaces land (signups today; future user-admin, security posture, etc.), routing every admin read through an audit-log bit obscures intent and blocks per-surface policy tuning (e.g. 'managers may view audit but NOT admin').

## Scope
- Add `admin.view` to `docs/specs/05-employees-and-roles.md` action catalog. Suggested: `valid_scope_kinds=('workspace','property','organization')`, `default_allow=('owners','managers')`, `root_protected_deny=True`. Matches the spec's 'operator-only' posture on /admin/signups and the governance posture of `audit_log.view`.
- Mirror the entry in `app/domain/identity/_action_catalog.py`.
- Switch `app/api/v1/admin/signups.py` (and any future workspace-admin routes) from `audit_log.view` to `admin.view`.
- Migration-path note: if any `permission_rule` rows have been written against `audit_log.view` specifically for the admin surface by the time this lands, rewrite them or document the one-time migration.

## Why not ship this in cd-g1ay
cd-g1ay was scoped to 'router scaffold + placeholder endpoint'. Adding a new action_key is a real spec edit (touches §05 + the catalog + the permission rule write-side) and warrants its own focused PR.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `admin.view` in the spec catalog + `_action_catalog.py`.
- [ ] `app/api/v1/admin/signups.py` depends on `Permission('admin.view', scope_kind='workspace')`.
- [ ] Tests cover the owner/manager default-allow + worker deny behaviour (already exists; retarget to the new key).
- [ ] Catalog-integrity test + OpenAPI doc pick up the new key automatically.

## Blocks / blocked by
- Blocked by: cd-g1ay (landed)
- Considered in: cd-ovt4 and any subsequent workspace-admin tasks — they should gate on `admin.view` once this lands.

title: flake: test_db_failure_is_swallowed_without_raising fails under full unit suite (PII redaction)
description:
## Problem

`tests/unit/auth/test_passkey_login.py::TestAutoRevokeCredentialFreshUow::test_db_failure_is_swallowed_without_raising` passes in isolation but fails under the full unit suite.

**Root cause (observed):** the test asserts the logged error message contains `cred_b64` (the credential id). When the suite runs end-to-end, something (likely an earlier test or the logging-config autouse fixture) wires in the PII redaction seam, which rewrites the credential id to `'***REDACTED***'` before the log record is captured. The assertion then fails:

```
assert any(cred_b64 in rec.getMessage() for rec in caplog.records)
```

Captured log shows `logger: app.api.v1.auth.passkey` record with `msg: "clone-detected auto-revoke failed on fresh UoW for id='***REDACTED***'"` — the id never reaches `caplog`.

**Confirmed pre-existing on main** (stashed cd-mb5n changes and reproduced on clean HEAD `9ec56a8`).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Fix the test so it either (a) asserts on a token that survives redaction (e.g. a correlation id), or (b) pins the redaction seam off for this specific test via the test fixture, or (c) asserts what the redaction pipeline actually emits (`***REDACTED***`).
- [ ] Confirm the fix holds under `pytest tests/unit -x -q` (the full unit suite order).
- [ ] Do not regress the isolation-level run.

## Refs

- `tests/unit/auth/test_passkey_login.py` `TestAutoRevokeCredentialFreshUow::test_db_failure_is_swallowed_without_raising`
- Relates to: cd-szxw, cd-0txr (prior un-flake attempt — commit `a365eef`).
- Discovered during cd-jkak selfreview of cd-mb5n.

title: authz: revoke x remove_member cross-path leaves owner without manager grant
description:
## Problem

The cd-mb5n lock closes the TOCTOU on the **count vs. write** step, but the `revoke` and `remove_member` guards still compose badly when one thread does each.

Scenario:
- Workspace has 2 owners (A, B), both with `manager` grants.
- Thread 1 calls `revoke(A-manager-grant)`; Thread 2 calls `remove_member(B from owners)`.
- cd-mb5n's lock serialises.
- Thread 1 acquires lock: counts 2 (A, B in owners). > 1, proceeds, revokes A's grant. Commits.
- Thread 2 acquires lock: counts 2 (A, B **still** in owners — thread 1 only revoked a grant, didn't touch membership). > 1, proceeds, removes B from owners. Commits.
- Final state: 1 owner (A) still in `owners@<ws>`, but **A no longer has the manager grant**. B holds the manager grant but is no longer an owner. The workspace has an owners anchor that can't administer, and an administrator who can't authorise as owner.

Same category as cd-nj8m: the cd-mb5n lock correctly serialises, but the guards check the wrong (too narrow) invariant.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Tie into cd-nj8m's resolution: whichever invariant is codified must hold across both `revoke` and `remove_member` paths.
- [ ] Guards must either count "live owners-with-manager-grant" (not just owners membership) OR the two services must emit a compensating check at the other side (e.g. `remove_member` on an owners group also checks the affected user has a replacement).
- [ ] Concurrency test covering `revoke` x `remove_member` cross-path race — add to `tests/integration/identity/test_last_owner_race.py` alongside the existing two scenarios.

## Refs

- cd-mb5n (TOCTOU-narrow fix)
- cd-nj8m (sibling — revoke × revoke hole; resolve together)
- `docs/specs/02-domain-model.md` §"permission_group" §"Invariants"
- `app/domain/identity/role_grants.py` `revoke`
- `app/domain/identity/permission_groups.py` `remove_member`
- `app/domain/identity/_owner_guard.py`

title: authz: revoke x revoke race leaves 2 owners with 0 manager grants (§02 invariant gap)
description:
## Problem

In `app.domain.identity.role_grants.revoke` the v1 last-owner guard compares `owner_count` (members of `owners@<ws>`) against the DELETE of a **manager role grant**. The two concerns are not the same invariant.

Scenario:
- Workspace has 2 owners (A, B), each with 1 `manager` grant.
- Two admins simultaneously call `revoke(A-manager-grant)` and `revoke(B-manager-grant)`.
- cd-mb5n's lock serialises them.
- Thread 1 acquires lock: counts 2 owners, > 1, proceeds to revoke A's grant. Commits.
- Thread 2 acquires lock: still counts 2 owners (membership hasn't changed — only grant was revoked). > 1, proceeds to revoke B's grant. Commits.
- Final state: 2 owners-members, **0 manager grants** — nobody can administer the workspace.

This is **not** a TOCTOU — even under perfect serialisation, the second revoke sees `count > 1` because the count is on owners-group **membership**, not on "owners who still carry a live manager grant".

The v1 guard's logic is narrower than §02's intent: §02 forbids an empty `owners` group but does not (yet) codify "at least one manager grant must remain on the workspace" as an invariant. Whether this scenario is allowed depends on how §02/§05 reconciles governance anchors with administrative surfaces.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Decide whether §02 or §05 should codify "at least one live `manager` grant on `owners@<ws>`" as an explicit invariant.
- [ ] Either update the spec then extend `role_grants.revoke` to count `(user in owners@<ws>) AND (user has live manager grant)` instead of just owners membership, OR document why this is an intentional governance choice.
- [ ] Concurrency test in `tests/integration/identity/test_last_owner_race.py` asserting the invariant (whichever way it's codified).
- [ ] Reuse the `count_owner_members_locked` helper if the rewrite keeps it shaped the same way; otherwise add a sibling in `_owner_guard.py`.

## Refs

- cd-mb5n (TOCTOU-narrow fix; this task is the wider governance gap)
- `docs/specs/02-domain-model.md` §"permission_group" §"Invariants"
- `docs/specs/05-tenancy-and-scope.md` §"Surface grants at a glance"
- `app/domain/identity/role_grants.py` `revoke`
- `app/domain/identity/_owner_guard.py`

title: chore(auth/signup): canonical /signup/passkey/finish lacks fresh-UoW challenge burn on failure
description:
## Problem / goal

The retired ``/auth/passkey/signup/register/finish`` router burned the
WebAuthn challenge row via ``_delete_challenge_fresh_uow`` on every
domain-level failure (cd-qx1f single-use-on-failure invariant). The
canonical ``/api/v1/signup/passkey/finish`` handler in
``app/api/v1/auth/signup.py`` (``post_passkey_finish`` at line ~702)
does NOT — its ``except _CompleteDomainError`` branch just re-raises
the HTTP exception without scheduling a fresh-UoW challenge delete.

Because ``complete_signup`` rolls back the primary UoW on a verify
failure (wrong signup_session_id / expired challenge / invalid
attestation), the challenge row survives the rollback and remains
redeemable until its 10-minute TTL. §03 "WebAuthn specifics" calls for
single-use even on failure. This was discovered during cd-ju0q's
retirement of the parallel ``/auth/passkey/signup/register/*`` surface
— the old path carried the burn, the canonical path inherits the gap.

## Technical context

- **Key files**: ``app/api/v1/auth/signup.py:702-735`` (handler),
  ``app/api/v1/auth/passkey.py`` (existing ``_delete_challenge_fresh_uow``
  helper — import and reuse rather than duplicate), ``app/auth/signup.py``
  (``complete_signup``'s raise points).
- **Spec**: ``docs/specs/03-auth-and-tokens.md`` §"WebAuthn specifics";
  cd-qx1f history for the invariant.
- **Existing coverage**: the retired router's burn-on-failure tests
  were deleted by cd-ju0q (``TestSignupFinishChallengeSingleUse`` in
  ``tests/unit/auth/test_passkey_router.py``). The canonical router
  currently has NO test for this invariant.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] ``post_passkey_finish`` burns the challenge row via
      ``_delete_challenge_fresh_uow`` (or an equivalent helper in
      this module) on every ``_CompleteDomainError`` branch that
      reaches a verified-and-failed state.
- [ ] New router-level test: failed finish leaves no
      ``WebAuthnChallenge`` row for the burned id (mirror the
      deleted ``TestSignupFinishChallengeSingleUse`` shape).
- [ ] Spec reference preserved — cd-qx1f / §03 "WebAuthn specifics".

## Out of scope

Login-side / additional-passkey burn paths are already correct;
this task only touches the canonical signup finish handler.

title: feat(api/web): server-render the SPA index template with CSP nonce seam
description:
## Problem / goal
Today `app/web/dist/index.html` is a static Vite build — no FastAPI involvement, and no inline scripts in the bootstrap (only external `src=...`). The strict CSP (`cd-wv2v`) accepts inline `<script>` / `<style>` only when tagged with the per-request `nonce-` value the middleware stamps into the CSP header.

The moment the SPA index becomes server-rendered (Jinja template, route handler streaming HTML, or SSR) to carry per-request state (CSRF token, nonce-protected inline bootstrap, flash banner, first-paint hydration data), inline tags must pick up `request.state.csp_nonce` or the browser will refuse to execute them.

## Scope
Swap the static `index.html` pass-through for a Jinja (or equivalent) template rendering path that:

- Reads the Vite-generated `dist/index.html`.
- Stamps `nonce={{ csp_nonce }}` onto every inline tag (today zero such tags — but the seam must be ready).
- Continues to serve the external-src scripts unchanged (no nonce needed there).
- Keeps the static file seam available for non-SPA paths (`/assets/*`, `/favicon.ico`, etc.).

- Spec: `docs/specs/14-web-frontend.md` §"Serving the SPA"; `docs/specs/15-security-privacy.md` §"HTTP security headers".
- Current static mount: `app/api/factory.py` (or wherever `StaticFiles` is wired for `app/web/dist/`).

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Request to `/` returns a Jinja-rendered index with the CSP nonce available in the template context
- [ ] Integration test: fetch `/`, parse the CSP header, confirm any inline `<script>` in the response carries `nonce="..."` matching
- [ ] `scripts/check_csp_inlines.py` runs against the rendered output (not just the Vite build) and stays green
- [ ] Static assets (`/assets/*`) still served directly

## Blocks (once this lands)
- cd-l2wa — stamping the nonce onto inline bootstrap tags (will just hook in once the template exists)

## Why file this now
cd-l2wa's own description says "trivially passes on main today (no server-rendered index exists)". Rather than carry cd-l2wa indefinitely in `bd ready`, file its prerequisite and link.

title: dev: flip mocks compose topology so HMR proxy is live
description:
## Problem / goal
The dev-profile Vite proxy (cd-q1be HTTP + cd-354g WebSocket) is now
fully unit-tested, but the **mocks/docker-compose.yml** topology
currently has the browser dial Vite directly on port 5173, bypassing
the FastAPI proxy. That means the WS HMR seam is never exercised by
a real browser in the dev stack — the unit tests are our only
evidence it works.

Flip the compose topology so the browser hits FastAPI (127.0.0.1:8100)
for everything, and FastAPI proxies to Vite. Once that lands, we can:

- Verify in the browser console that editing `app/web/src/**/*.tsx`
  triggers `[vite] hot updated` (not `connection lost`) — the
  cd-354g acceptance criterion we couldn't tick off.
- Close the only remaining gap between the proxy's unit coverage and
  real-browser behaviour.

## Technical context
- **Current topology (mocks/docker-compose.yml)**: browser → Vite:5173
  directly; FastAPI only serves /api on :8100. See the container
  service definitions and the README under `mocks/`.
- **Desired topology**: browser → FastAPI:8100; FastAPI dev proxy →
  Vite:5173 (loopback between containers). Matches how prod will
  serve the compiled SPA and the API from a single origin.
- `vite.config.ts` already carries the proxy definitions for
  `/api`, `/events`, etc. — flipping the browser entrypoint to
  FastAPI is the missing piece.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Browser loads `http://127.0.0.1:8100/` and receives Vite's
      index via the FastAPI proxy
- [ ] Editing a .tsx triggers `[vite] hot updated` in the console
- [ ] `/api/*` + `/events` continue to land on real FastAPI
      routers (the new `_is_non_spa_path` guard)
- [ ] README under `mocks/` updated with the new flow

Discovered via cd-r4uq self-review of cd-354g.

title: Self-review: feat(api/inventory): stocktake endpoints + paginated per-item history + new reports
description:
## Problem / goal
Auto-fixing self-review of the changes made under cd-cngj. Catch
bugs, missing pieces, and unintended consequences before they ship.

**Depends on: cd-cngj** (main task must be complete first).

## How to run
Run ``/selfreview`` in **autofix mode** against the commit(s) from
cd-cngj.

- Do NOT enter plan mode.
- Do NOT ask the user to triage findings — you are the triage.
- Apply fixes for every BUGS, MISSING, and RISKY finding.
- Skip NITPICKS unless trivially safe.
- Particular focus: cursor-pagination invariants under concurrent
  writes (no dupes, no gaps), idempotency-key coverage on
  commit, OpenAPI/Schemathesis parity, decimal-precision round
  trip through JSON.
- Commit the fixes, push, and close this task.

See ``.claude/skills/selfreview/SKILL.md``.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] All BUGS from the self-review fixed
- [ ] All MISSING pieces completed
- [ ] All RISKY items mitigated (or justified in a task comment)
- [ ] Linter, formatter, type checker, affected tests all pass
- [ ] Schemathesis stays green for tag ``inventory``
- [ ] Fixes committed and pushed
